text:
**T4 — THE PROOF ITSELF ("The transcript is the headline")**
- Hero: **"@codex, take the failing tests — I've got the migration."** — set as a literal terminal transcript, with the beat underneath: *Typed in Claude Code. Landed in Codex's terminal as a real turn.*
- Subhead: "tenex-edge gives the agents you already run — Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Grok — durable identities and a shared awareness fabric. They see what each other is doing and message each other directly. You stop hand-carrying."
- Bet: pure show-don't-tell. The single most surprising shipped fact IS the headline; zero adjectives, all demonstration. For an audience that punishes hype and rewards proof, this is maximally disarming.
- Wins: everyone who sees it move (the GIF version is undeniable); the hype-allergic reader who bounces off any claim-shaped headline. Loses: risks reading as a party trick if the identity/fabric substrate isn't seated *immediately* below; slightly undersells the durability moat in second one. [...] **Territory: T4 (the proof) as the hero, carrying T1's pain in the subhead and T2's identity as the immediate second beat. Metaphor: the fabric, with "the host is a body; the identity is the person" as the signature line and citizenship demoted to one earned use in Beat 5.**

Rationale: this audience's stated allergy is claims and its stated currency is proof — and this product owns the one demo in the entire 90-tool category that no one else can screenshot: a message typed in Claude Code arriving in Codex's live terminal as a real turn. Opening with that transcript makes the first impression *undeniable rather than arguable*, which no pain-hook or identity-thesis headline can achieve against a reader trained to discount both. The structural risk of T4 (party-trick misreading) is fully neutralized by the architecture: identity-as-foundation is Beat 4's job and the subhead already carries "durable identities," so the moat is staked within the first screen. T1 and T2 remain alive as the subhead and Beat 2/4 respectively — nothing is lost; the order is simply proof → pain → floor → moat, which is the exact sequence in which this specific reader lowers their guard.

One-line compass for the writing pass: **every claim must be screenshotable, every horizon must be labeled, and the word "orchestration" must never appear.** [...] **Three decisions that shape everything:**
1. **Proof-first, not claim-first.** The hero is the one demo nobody else can screenshot — an `@mention` typed in Claude Code landing in Codex's live terminal as a real turn. This audience discounts headlines and rewards demonstration.
2. **Identity is the anchor, "clobbering" is only the hook.** Your own doctrine distrusts collision/coordination as narrow and unproven, so I lead the *felt* pain in the subhead but stake the durable-identity moat as beat two — and put "collision detection" in the honest *what-this-isn't* section. [...] ═══════════════════════════════════════

**Beat 1 — Above the fold:** Hero headline + the @mention terminal transcript/GIF (per §6, they're the same thing) + one-sentence definition using the category label + install one-liner. The proof is not below the fold; the proof IS the fold.

**Beat 2 — The pain, in their words (3-4 sentences max).** "Agent A refactors a function. Agent B, unaware, edits the same function. You route the work, check the overlap, decide the merge order, tell each one what to retry. The more agents you run, the worse it gets." Then the turn: "The agents aren't the bottleneck. The bus between them is — and the bus is you."

**Beat 3 — What ships today.** Plain bullet list, all present tense, all verified: durable identity per (agent, machine) · presence/liveness · LLM-distilled one-line activity broadcast each turn · session-targeted @mentions injected as real turns · one daemon, one store, one relay connection · verified live on Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Grok. This section is the credibility spine; nothing aspirational may touch it.

**Beat 4 — Why identity is the foundation (the anchor).** The "host is a body" paragraph. Why ephemeral session IDs can't do this. **The Agent Teams comparison lands HERE as a small honest table** (host-neutral? cross-session? cross-machine? self-owned identity?) alongside hcom and mcp_agent_mail — T3's argument at its correct altitude: beat 4, not beat 1.

**Beat 5 — How it works.** Hooks ("the plugin is just the straw — tenex-edge knows nothing about the host"), the daemon, SQLite, fail-open design, the corruption war story in one sentence. **"Built on Nostr" is revealed here and only here**, as three property bullets, then the protocol name. The single permitted use of "citizenship for your agents" closes this section.

**Beat 6 — What this isn't (yet).** Its own honest heading. Cross-person agents: gated behind a trust model we haven't built. Collision detection: a hypothesis, explicitly not the product's identity. The agent-society sketches: illustrative horizon. This section is a *feature* for this audience — it's the anti-hype signature move, and its existence is what licenses the confidence everywhere else.

**Beat 7 — Quickstart per host + FAQ** (first FAQ entry: "How is this different from Claude Code Agent Teams?" — restate beat 4 in two sentences). [...] ## Deliverables

**1. `README.md` — rewritten from scratch** (in repo, replacing the old one). Follows the committed 7-beat arc: proof hero → the pain in their words → what ships today (present-tense, tested only) → why identity is the foundation + an honest comparison table vs. Agent Teams / hcom / mcp_agent_mail → how it works (Nostr revealed here) → *what this isn't yet* → quickstart/commands/FAQ. Every claim is grounded in the code-verified brief. I also **killed a stale claim**: the old README advertised a `tail` command that has since been removed (there's a test asserting it's gone) — replaced with `who --live`.
 [...] **T1 — THE FELT PAIN ("Resign as message bus")**
- Hero: **"Stop being the message bus between your own agents."**
- Subhead: "You run Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode on the same repo — and you hand-carry every decision between them. tenex-edge lets them see what each other is doing and @mention each other directly, in the terminals they already run in."
- Bet: maximum resonance; it's a near-verbatim quote of the audience's own words. Wins the HN skimmer instantly.
- Wins: anyone who has felt the pain (most of the audience). Loses: doesn't stake the identity moat above the fold; pain-first headlines are a known genre, so it's the least *ownable* of the four; invites "so it's a chat tool?" misreading. [...] **README (`README.md`):**
- **Gave it a real headline** — `## Stop being the message bus between your own agents.` right after the transcript; split the overloaded intro.
- **Handled the trust objection** — the `@mention` bullet now states the fabric is yours-only, plus three new FAQ entries: *who can message my agents* (membership-gated, unrecognized senders quarantined), *whose LLM reads my transcript* (the provider **you** configure; keys never leave disk), and a **risk-reversal**: `bash scripts/demo.sh` runs the whole loop on your machine. [...] 
P2-15. Harmonize the pain headline: README "The problem is the wire, and the wire is you" vs landing "The problem is the wire. The wire is you." The landing's two-sentence version is punchier — adopt in README.
 [...] - **HTTPS clone URLs** (both were SSH — broke copy-paste for anyone without GitHub keys).
- Ported the cleaner landing lines back ("You're owed the boundary", "customs office"); table cell → "central registry"; harmonized the drifted pain-headline and metaphor.
 [...] 
**Beat 4 — Why identity is the foundation (the anchor).** The "host is a body" paragraph. Why ephemeral session IDs can't do this. **The Agent Teams comparison lands HERE as a small honest table** (host-neutral? cross-session? cross-machine? self-owned identity?) alongside hcom and mcp_agent_mail — T3's argument at its correct altitude: beat 4, not beat 1.
 [...] ## Two things worth your call

- **The comparison table names competitors.** It's the sharpest anti-"worse Agent Teams" move, but competitor feature sets move fast — I labeled it "mid-2026 snapshot." Say the word if you'd rather soften it to unnamed categories.
- **The hero wants a real GIF/asciinema** of the actual injection to replace the static transcript — that's the single highest-leverage asset for launch. I can script `scripts/demo.sh` into a recordable capture if you want it. [...] 1. **License.** The reviewer suggested a "MIT / open source" footer line — but there is **no LICENSE file and no `license` field in Cargo.toml**, so the repo is currently *all-rights-reserved*. I did **not** add the claim (it'd be false). This is a genuine launch blocker for an OSS-positioned tool: **you'll want to add a license before the README implies open source.** Want me to add one (MIT/Apache-2.0)?
2. **The competitor-comparison table names names.** Still the sharpest anti-"worse Agent Teams" move; still labeled "mid-2026 snapshot." Left as-is unless you'd rather soften to unnamed categories.
 [...] 
P2-11. Table microcopy "❌ registered names" (README:102) / "named" (landing). Cryptic — the reader can't tell why "named" is a ✕. Rewrite the cell: "central registry" — that's the actual contrast with "keys on disk."
 [...] - **HTTPS clone URLs** (both were SSH — broke copy-paste for anyone without GitHub keys).
- Ported the cleaner landing lines back ("You're owed the boundary", "customs office"); table cell → "central registry"; harmonized the drifted pain-headline and metaphor.
 [...] 
**Beat 6 — What this isn't (yet).** Its own honest heading. Cross-person agents: gated behind a trust model we haven't built. Collision detection: a hypothesis, explicitly not the product's identity. The agent-society sketches: illustrative horizon. This section is a *feature* for this audience — it's the anti-hype signature move, and its existence is what licenses the confidence everywhere else.
 [...] 2. **Identity is the anchor, "clobbering" is only the hook.** Your own doctrine distrusts collision/coordination as narrow and unproven, so I lead the *felt* pain in the subhead but stake the durable-identity moat as beat two — and put "collision detection" in the honest *what-this-isn't* section.
3. **"Nostr / crypto / decentralized" never appears above the architecture section** (crypto dev mindshare is collapsing and "Nostr + AI agent" is already colonized by token plays). Above the fold it's described by properties — *keys on your disk, no central server, works if a relay dies*. Nostr is revealed once, as a credibility signal in "How it works." [...] 
P1-8. README:134: "This audience is owed the boundary, so here it is, plainly." Talks ABOUT the reader in third person, to the reader. Skill: Customer Language / directness. The landing already has the right version — port it: "You're owed the boundary. Here it is."
 [...] - **HTTPS clone URLs** (both were SSH — broke copy-paste for anyone without GitHub keys).
- Ported the cleaner landing lines back ("You're owed the boundary", "customs office"); table cell → "central registry"; harmonized the drifted pain-headline and metaphor.
 [...] 
P1-9. README:138–139: "Opening the borders before the customs office works is how you ship the dangerous thing first." Convoluted negation-of-a-negation; "the dangerous thing" is vague. Skill: Clarity Over Cleverness. The landing version is the fix: "You build the customs office before you open the borders."
 [...] - **HTTPS clone URLs** (both were SSH — broke copy-paste for anyone without GitHub keys).
- Ported the cleaner landing lines back ("You're owed the boundary", "customs office"); table cell → "central registry"; harmonized the drifted pain-headline and metaphor.
 [...] P0-4. Quickstart clone URL is SSH: `git clone git@github.com:pablof7z/tenex-edge.git` (README lines 23 and 156).
The copy-paste conversion path fails for anyone without GitHub SSH keys — friction at the exact moment of conversion. Skill: Be Direct / reduce friction at the CTA.
Rewrite: `git clone https://github.com/pablof7z/tenex-edge.git`.
 [...] 3. **Align the CLI `about` string + Cargo `description`** — both currently lead with "Citizenship… on the Nostr fabric," violating the demoted-metaphor and no-Nostr-above-the-fold rules on a *first-touch* surface (`--help`). → *"An identity and awareness fabric for the coding agents you already run."* (move `nostr`/`nip29` to Cargo `keywords`).
4. **HTTPS the clone URL** (`git@github.com:…` → `https://…`) — SSH breaks copy-paste for anyone without GitHub keys, right at the conversion moment.
 [...] - **Active voice** on the flagship feature (*"an LLM distills each turn… and broadcasts it"*).
- **HTTPS clone URLs** (both were SSH — broke copy-paste for anyone without GitHub keys).
- Ported the cleaner landing lines back ("You're owed the boundary", "customs office"); table cell → "central registry"; harmonized the drifted pain-headline and metaphor. [...] P0-3. The trust-model objection is never handled.
"@mention delivered as a real turn" is, framed adversarially, remote prompt injection into your agent — and this audience WILL frame it that way. `whitelistedPubkeys` appears once as raw config, never as a promise. Violates: the skill's Objection Handling core section (Page Structure Framework).
Fix: one clause in the @mention feature cell — "Only keys you whitelist can reach your agents." — plus a README FAQ entry: "Can anyone message my agents?" → "No. The daemon only acts on events signed by pubkeys you whitelist in ~/.tenex-edge/config.json." Same treatment for the activity line: one sentence on which LLM distills, whose tokens, where the sentence is published.
 [...] - **Gave it a real headline** — `## Stop being the message bus between your own agents.` right after the transcript; split the overloaded intro.
- **Handled the trust objection** — the `@mention` bullet now states the fabric is yours-only, plus three new FAQ entries: *who can message my agents* (membership-gated, unrecognized senders quarantined), *whose LLM reads my transcript* (the provider **you** configure; keys never leave disk), and a **risk-reversal**: `bash scripts/demo.sh` runs the whole loop on your machine.
- **Active voice** on the flagship feature (*"an LLM distills each turn… and broadcasts it"*). [...] - Social proof (logos, stars, testimonials): absent, and CORRECTLY so — padding would violate Honest over sensational and this audience punishes it. The transcript, "374 green," and the named-competitor table are the substitute, and better.
- Risk reversal: missing, and there's a free one in the repo — reproducible proof. "Don't take the transcript's word for it — `bash scripts/demo.sh` runs two agents mentioning each other on your machine." That is the dev-native form of a money-back guarantee (skill: Final CTA + risk reversal). One line near the quickstart or footer CTA.
- Objection handling on the landing: the real gap (P0-3, P1-10). Priority order for this reader: (1) who can inject turns into my agent, (2) where do my transcript summaries go / whose LLM tokens, (3) what breaks when the daemon/relay dies (already answered in README FAQ — surface on the landing too). [...] - **Gave it a real headline** — `## Stop being the message bus between your own agents.` right after the transcript; split the overloaded intro.
- **Handled the trust objection** — the `@mention` bullet now states the fabric is yours-only, plus three new FAQ entries: *who can message my agents* (membership-gated, unrecognized senders quarantined), *whose LLM reads my transcript* (the provider **you** configure; keys never leave disk), and a **risk-reversal**: `bash scripts/demo.sh` runs the whole loop on your machine.
- **Active voice** on the flagship feature (*"an LLM distills each turn… and broadcasts it"*). [...] - Added trust clause to the `@mention` card; active voice on distill; demoted the "straw/milkshake" riddle out of a cold heading.
- **Added a "Common questions" strip** before the final CTA (the biggest missing conversion element per the skill) — who-can-message / whose-LLM / relay-dies / the demo risk-reversal.
 [...] 
P1-10. Landing has zero objection-handling section. Structure runs pain → ships → identity → how → isn't → CTA. "What this isn't" handles SCOPE objections brilliantly, but the skill's strong-page template (copy-frameworks: "Varied, Engaging Page") wants FAQ/objections before the final CTA. A three-question strip (who can message my agents / which LLM & whose tokens / what if the relay dies) or a "Common questions →" link to the README FAQ closes it.
 [...] - Added trust clause to the `@mention` card; active voice on distill; demoted the "straw/milkshake" riddle out of a cold heading.
- **Added a "Common questions" strip** before the final CTA (the biggest missing conversion element per the skill) — who-can-message / whose-LLM / relay-dies / the demo risk-reversal.
